1: A theme: Naomi seeks mānôaḥ — a settled resting place — for Ruth. The word carries the Sabbath-rest theme that runs from creation (Genesis 2:2-3) through the conquest (Joshua 21:44) into the prophets’ eschatological promise (Isaiah 28:12; Jeremiah 6:16). Ruth needs more than gleaning rights; she needs the security of a covenant household. Christ offers exactly this: “Come to me... and I will give you rest” (Matthew 11:28) — not the rest of inactivity but the settled security of belonging to the redeemer.
2: A shadow: Boaz is winnowing barley at the threshing floor tonight. The threshing floor is the OT’s primary image for judgment and sorting — the separation of grain from chaff. John the Baptist uses exactly this image for Christ’s coming: “his winnowing fork is in his hand, and he will clear his threshing floor and gather his wheat into the barn, but the chaff he will burn with unquenchable fire” (Matthew 3:12). The threshing floor where Ruth asks for redemption is the space where the eschatological Redeemer will accomplish final separation.
3: A shadow: Naomi’s instructions to Ruth — wash, anoint yourself, put on your best garments — are the preparation of the bride for meeting the one who may redeem her. The same preparation language governs the vision of the new Jerusalem: “the bride has made herself ready... arrayed in fine linen, bright and pure” (Revelation 19:7-8). The church comes to Christ prepared by the Spirit’s work, presenting herself in deliberate readiness.
4: A shadow: “Uncover his feet and lie down, and he will tell you what to do.” The posture at the redeemer’s feet — vulnerable, waiting for his word — is the posture of those who meet Jesus: Mary sitting at his feet (Luke 10:39), the woman washing his feet with tears (Luke 7:38), the disciples after the resurrection falling at his feet (Matthew 28:9). The one who lies at the redeemer’s feet awaits his word; that word will be good news.
5: A theme: “I will do everything you say.” Ruth’s complete trust in Naomi’s instruction — even when the instruction is socially risky and its logic unexplained — models the faith that follows the covenant mediator’s direction without demanding prior explanation. The disciples’ immediate response to Jesus’s call (Matthew 4:20: “immediately they left their nets and followed”) has the same shape: trust precedes understanding.
6: A theme: Ruth goes and does exactly as instructed — complete obedience before the outcome is known. Christ’s own obedience to the Father is the model: “although he was a son, he learned obedience through what he suffered” (Hebrews 5:8); “not my will, but yours, be done” (Luke 22:42). The pattern of faith is: hear, trust, obey — in that order, without requiring prior knowledge of the result.
7: A theme: Boaz’s heart is glad after eating and drinking — the harvest complete, provision made — and he lies down at the far end of the grain heap. The redeemer at rest after the work is complete anticipates Christ who, having accomplished the work the Father gave him, “sat down at the right hand of the Majesty on high” (Hebrews 1:3). The redeemer’s rest after completed work signals not abandonment but the perfection of what was done.
8: A theme: at midnight Boaz startles and finds a woman lying at his feet. The midnight encounter — the one who waits in the right posture for the redeemer’s discovery — echoes the parable of the ten virgins: “at midnight there was a cry, Here is the bridegroom!” (Matthew 25:6). Those found waiting at the redeemer’s feet are received; those who are not in position at the critical moment find the door shut.
9: A type: “Spread your garment over your servant, for you are a kinsman-redeemer.” Ruth takes the wing-imagery of Ruth 2:12 — “under whose wings you have come to take refuge” — and asks Boaz to be its physical embodiment. Ezekiel 16:8 uses the same gesture for YHWH’s covenant with Israel: “I spread the corner of my garment over you and you became mine.” The redeemer who shelters under YHWH’s wings becomes the wing for those who shelter in him. In Christ, YHWH’s protective love takes flesh.
10: A theme: Boaz says Ruth’s latter act of hesed is greater than the former — loyalty that perseveres beyond the convenient. Christ honors persistent, exceeding faith: the centurion (“not even in Israel have I found such faith” — Matthew 8:10), the Syrophoenician woman’s refusal to accept the first answer (Matthew 15:28), the widow’s two coins (Mark 12:44). The hesed that goes beyond what circumstance demands is the form of faith the redeemer most explicitly commends.
11: A revelation of God: “All in the gate know you are a woman of worth (ʾēšet ḥayil).” The public community testifies to the outsider’s character — the one who entered as a Moabite is now known by her hesed. Christ names his own publicly before his Father (Matthew 10:32: “I will also acknowledge before my Father who is in heaven”). The redeemer endorses the character of those who follow him; the community’s witness is the social form of that endorsement.
12: A theme: Boaz acknowledges the nearer kinsman honestly. He does not hide the legal complexity or skip the required procedure. The redeemer is fully transparent about what stands between the petition and redemption’s completion. Christ likewise fulfills the law’s requirements completely rather than bypassing them (Matthew 5:17: “I have not come to abolish the Law and the Prophets but to fulfill them”).
13: A type: “As the LORD lives, I will redeem you.” Boaz’s vow by YHWH’s own life is the ground of certainty for Ruth’s hope. The redeemer’s commitment is secured by the eternal life of God. The writer of Hebrews makes the same argument for Christ’s priesthood: his intercession is permanent because he “holds his priesthood permanently, because he continues forever” (Hebrews 7:24). Ruth can rest at the redeemer’s feet because his vow is sworn on the life that cannot end.
14: A theme: Boaz sends Ruth away before dawn to protect her reputation. The redeemer guards the honor of the one he is in process of redeeming. Christ’s intercessory work is this same ongoing protection: “he always lives to make intercession for them” (Hebrews 7:25). The period between the promise of redemption and its completion is not a time of exposure but of active intercession.
15: A shadow: six measures of barley given before the transaction closes — the redeemer’s pledge of intention. The earnest payment before full redemption mirrors the Spirit as the “pledge (arrabōn) of our inheritance until we acquire full possession of it” (Ephesians 1:14; 2 Corinthians 1:22). The gift before the completion demonstrates that the redeemer’s commitment is real; the Spirit given now guarantees the fullness still to come.
16: A theme: Ruth reports everything Boaz said and did. The witness who returns from the redeemer’s presence and tells the full story is the NT disciple’s role: “we cannot but speak of what we have seen and heard” (Acts 4:20). Testimony to the redeemer’s words and actions — exact content, specific character — is the form that gospel proclamation takes in every generation.
17: A theme: “He gave me these six measures of barley with the instruction not to go back empty-handed.” The redeemer sends provision back to those not yet in his presence. Naomi receives from Ruth what Boaz gave — grace transmitted through the one who has been with the redeemer to those not yet reached. The gospel’s transmission follows this pattern: the community that has met Christ carries his provision to those not yet brought near.
18: A theme: “Stay still, my daughter, until you know how the matter will turn out. The man will not rest until the matter is settled today.” The counsel to “stay still” while the redeemer acts is the posture of those who have received the earnest of the Spirit while awaiting the fullness of redemption. “Be still and know that I am God” (Psalms 46:10); “we ourselves groan inwardly as we wait eagerly for adoption” (Romans 8:23). Naomi’s confidence in the redeemer’s urgency is confidence that he “will bring it to completion” (Philippians 1:6).
